student_message: That makes so much sense! A slice of pizza has just one fold line because the left and right match. Why do some things like the letter 'S' look balanced but have zero fold lines?
final_message: I love your pizza slice example, Zara! You are doing amazing! 🍕 The letter 'S' is super tricky. It looks beautifully balanced because if you spin it upside down, it looks exactly the same. But remember our magic fold line test? To have reflection symmetry, the two halves must be perfect mirror images. If we try to fold an 'S' right down the middle, the top curve loops one way and the bottom curve loops the other way. When we fold the paper, those curves will not land perfectly on top of each other. The exact same thing happens if we try to fold it top to bottom! So even though it is a very balanced letter, it cannot pass the fold test to have a line of symmetry. ✨ What other letters do you think might trick us like that?

orchestrator_intent.goal: Explain why the letter 'S' lacks reflection symmetry despite looking balanced.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: Zara is happy, focused, and asked a specific conceptual question. The dialogue agent is best suited to explain.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.
